name: Sierra de Juan Robre from Arenas de Cabrales — circular in the Parque Nacional Picos de Europa
name_original: Sierra de Juan Robre desde Arenas de Cabrales — circular en el Parque Nacional Picos de Europa
region_intro: Hiking around Cabrales offers access to the Picos de Europa National Park, characterized by towering limestone peaks, deep gorges, and lush valleys. The region's unique karst system and steep orography create dramatic geological formations. This terrain provides diverse hiking experiences, from challenging ascents to paths through charming villages. Best hiking trails around Cabrales The most popular hiking route is Route of Cares from Poncebos to Caín, a 14.4 miles (23.1 km) trail that takes 7 hours 30 minutes to complete. This route carves through the Cares Gorge, offering views of the river and rugged limestone cliffs. Another top favourite among local hikers is Bulnes de Arriba, Bulnes de Abajo, Mirador del Urriellu desde Poncebos — Parque Nacional Picos de Europa, a difficult 5.9 miles (9.5 km) path. This trail provides perspectives of the iconic Picu Urriellu and passes through the picturesque village of Bulnes. Local hikers also love the Refugio de la Terenosa y Refugio Urriellu desde el Collado de Pandébano — Parque Nacional Picos de Europa, a 7.9 miles (12.7 km) trail leading through high mountain terrain, often completed in about 5 hours 45 minutes. Hiking around Cabrales is defined by limestone peaks, deep gorges, and a unique karst system. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate walks to challenging mountaineering routes. The routes in Cabrales are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 2900 reviews. More than 18000 hikers have used komoot to explore Cabrales's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when hiking in Cabrales?
A: Hiking in Cabrales means exploring the Picos de Europa National Park, characterized by its dramatic limestone peaks, deep gorges, and lush valleys. The region's unique karst system creates steep, dizzying slopes and impressive geological formations, offering a diverse range of experiences from challenging ascents to paths through charming villages.

Q: Are there any easy or family-friendly hiking options in Cabrales?
A: Yes, while Cabrales is known for its challenging routes, there are also easier options. The region offers over 30 easy tours suitable for those looking for a more relaxed experience or hiking with family. These trails often provide stunning views without the extreme difficulty of the higher mountain paths.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions or viewpoints to see while hiking in Cabrales?
A: Cabrales is rich in natural beauty and offers numerous viewpoints. You can experience the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6051790" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cares Gorge&lt;/a&gt;, or visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/280873" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Cable Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; for panoramic vistas. The region's karst system also features crystal-clear streams, such as one found near the Cares River (&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3289009" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Crystal-clear stream near the Cares River&lt;/a&gt;), adding to the scenic variety.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Cabrales?
A: Yes, Cabrales offers several excellent circular routes. One popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1192616797/tielve-majada-de-valfrio-majada-de-tobaos-monte-camba-sotres-circular-en-el-parque-nacional-picos-de-europa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tielve, Majada de Valfrío, Majada de Tobaos, Monte Camba, Sotres — circular en el Parque Nacional Picos de Europa&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through picturesque villages and offers spectacular views. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1208982385/sierra-de-juan-robre-from-arenas-de-cabrales-circular-in-the-parque-nacional-picos-de-europa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Juan Robre from Arenas de Cabrales&lt;/a&gt;, starting from the central hub of Arenas de Cabrales.

Q: What is the best time of year to hike in Cabrales?
A: The best time to hike in Cabrales is generally from late spring to early autumn (May to October) when the weather is most stable and trails are clear of snow. Summer offers warmer temperatures, but spring and autumn provide pleasant conditions with fewer crowds and vibrant landscapes. Winter hiking is possible but requires specialized gear and experience due to snow and ice, especially at higher elevations.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Cabrales?
A: Many trails in the Picos de Europa National Park, including those around Cabrales, are dog-friendly. However, it's crucial to keep dogs on a leash to protect local wildlife and livestock, especially in grazing areas. Always check specific trail regulations before heading out, as some protected areas might have restrictions.

Q: What is the difficulty level of the 'Refugio de la Terenosa y Refugio Urriellu' route?
A: The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1181698750/refugio-de-la-terenosa-y-refugio-urriellu-desde-el-collado-de-pandebano-parque-nacional-picos-de-europa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Refugio de la Terenosa y Refugio Urriellu desde el Collado de Pandébano&lt;/a&gt; is classified as a difficult trail. It covers 7.9 miles (12.7 km) with significant elevation gain, leading through high mountain terrain. This route is best suited for experienced hikers with good physical condition.

Q: Are there any villages or towns that serve as good starting points for hikes in Cabrales?
A: Absolutely. &lt;strong&gt;Arenas de Cabrales&lt;/strong&gt; is a central hub, offering amenities and easy access to many routes. &lt;strong&gt;Poncebos&lt;/strong&gt; is another critical access point, especially for the Cares Route and the funicular to Bulnes. &lt;strong&gt;Sotres&lt;/strong&gt;, as the highest village in the Picos, is a lively mountaineering village from which many routes begin or pass through, offering magnificent views.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Cabrales?
A: The hiking routes in Cabrales are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 2900 reviews. Hikers frequently praise the dramatic scenery, the unique karst topography, and the variety of trails that cater to different skill levels, from moderate walks to challenging mountaineering routes.

Q: Is parking available at the trailheads in Cabrales?
A: Parking is generally available at popular trailheads and in the main villages like Arenas de Cabrales, Poncebos, and Sotres. However, during peak season, parking spaces can fill up quickly, especially for very popular routes like the Cares Route. It's advisable to arrive early or consider public transport options where available.

Q: How many hiking routes are available in the Cabrales region?
A: There are over 170 hiking routes available in the Cabrales region, offering a wide variety of experiences. This includes 30 easy routes, 57 moderate routes, and 83 difficult routes, ensuring options for all levels of hikers.

Q: What is the 'Bulnes de Arriba, Bulnes de Abajo, Mirador del Urriellu' route like?
A: The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1190209824/bulnes-de-arriba-bulnes-de-abajo-mirador-del-urriellu-desde-poncebos-parque-nacional-picos-de-europa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bulnes de Arriba, Bulnes de Abajo, Mirador del Urriellu desde Poncebos&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 5.9-mile (9.5 km) path. This trail provides stunning perspectives of the iconic Picu Urriellu and takes you through the picturesque, formerly isolated village of Bulnes, offering a unique cultural and scenic experience.
tips: Arenas de Cabrales, known as Las Arenas, is famous worldwide for being the place of origin of Cabrales cheese and is one of the most outstanding accesses to the Picos de Europa National Park.
Cabeza Juan Robre (876 m) has a beautiful view over Arenas de Cabrales and the valley of La Ardina.
The Jucelagüelga flock is between Cabeza Juan Robre and Cabeza Turueco. From the Jucelagüelga cabins you can see the ascent to the westernmost summit of this Juan de Robre mountain range.
A welcome fountain where you can fill your water bottles and cool off a bit.
Enjoy the beautiful green hills and views. There are some dilapidated huts and you will probably see cattle and other animals grazing.
A GPS is useful on top of the mountain. The red signposts are not so clearly visible.

waypoints.name: View from La Cabeza Juan Robre
